content.problemStatement:
There exists a hidden array AAA of length NNN such that there is an integer XXX (1≤X≤N1 \le X \le N1≤X≤N) satisfying


Ai=0A_i = 0Ai​=0 for i<Xi < Xi<X
Ai=1A_i = 1Ai​=1 for i≥Xi \ge Xi≥X
In other words, the array consists of some number of 000's followed by only 111's, and XXX is the position of the first 111.

In particular, AAA definitely contains at least one occurrence of the value 111.

You do not know anything else about the array or the integer XXX.

Suppose you are allowed to query the array at most QQQ times.

In a query, you must choose an index jjj (1≤j≤N1 \le j \le N1≤j≤N) and the process behaves as follows:


You are told the value of AjA_jAj​.
If Aj=1A_j = 1Aj​=1, the process **terminates immediately** even if less than QQQ queries have been used.

Otherwise, you may continue to use more queries (if you have any left).
After the process terminates, you must provide a guess GGG for the value of XXX.

The error of the guess is defined as ∣G−X∣|G - X|∣G−X∣.

What is the **minimum possible worst-case error** you can guarantee if you choose your queries and final guess optimally?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of single line of input, two integers NNN and QQQ.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum error that can be guaranteed.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: For N=2,Q=0N = 2, Q = 0N=2,Q=0 we have no queries available to give us any information, and so must guess immediately.

If we guess G=1G = 1G=1, we get an error of 000 when X=1X = 1X=1 and an error of 111 if X=2X = 2X=2.
If we guess G=2G = 2G=2, we get an error of 111 when X=1X = 1X=1 and an error of 000 if X=2X = 2X=2.
Neither guess can guarantee a worst-case error that's better than 111, so the answer is 111.

Test case 222: For N=2,Q=1N = 2, Q = 1N=2,Q=1, we can query A1A_1A1​. Then, if A1=1A_1 = 1A1​=1 then X=1X = 1X=1 is guaranteed, and if A1=0A_1 = 0A1​=0 then X=2X = 2X=2 is guaranteed; so in either case we are able to precisely determine XXX, hence obtaining an error of 000.
Test case 333: For N=3,Q=1N = 3, Q = 1N=3,Q=1, one strategy is as follows:

Query A1A_1A1​.
If A1=1A_1 = 1A1​=1 then we know X=1X = 1X=1 for sure.
If A1=0A_1 = 0A1​=0 then we can guess G=2G = 2G=2 for a worst-case error of 111.
